What Are Mail-In Ballots?

Mail-in ballots are electronic votes cast by citizens who do not physically visit a polling place but instead request their vote through digital means. They allow voters to choose whether to submit a paper ballot or use the electronic system. In the event of an election day shortage, mail-in ballots provide a safe haven for those unable to physically vote.

For decades, mail-in ballots have been essential in safeguarding the integrity of our democratic processes. They serve as a critical safeguard against voter fraud and ensure that every eligible voter has equal access to participating in the democratic process. By providing an alternative method of voting, mail-in ballots prevent the spread of misinformation and promote fair representation.
